author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew V. 38, 39, 40.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I say unto you, that ye resist not the evil:but whosoever shall smite thee on the right cheek, turn to him the other also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou that it was not of an eye that He was speaking before, when He made the law to pluck out the offending eye, but of him who by his friendship is harming us, and casting us into the gulf of destruction?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He who in this place uses so great strength of expression, and who, not even when another is plucking out your eye, permits you to strike out his; how should He have made it a law to strike out one’s own?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if any one accuses the ancient law, because it commands such retaliation, he seems to me very unskillful in the wisdom that becomes a legislator, and ignorant of the virtue of opportunities, and the gain of condescension.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he considered who were the hearers of these sayings, and how they were disposed, and when they received this code of laws, he will thoroughly admit the wisdom of the Lawgiver, and will see that it is one and the same, who made both those laws and these, and who wrote each of them exceeding profitably, and in its due season.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for if at the beginning He had introduced these high and most weighty commandments, men would not have received either these, or the others; but now ordaining them severally in their due time, He hath by the two corrected the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, He commanded this, not that we might strike out one another’s eyes, but that we might keep our hands to ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the threat of suffering hath effectually restrained our inclination to be doing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thus in fact He is silently dropping seed of much self-restraint, at least in that He commands to retaliate with just the same acts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely he that began such transgression were worthy of a greater punishment, and this the abstract nature of justicedemands.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But forasmuch as He was minded to mingle mercy also with justice, He condemns him whose offenses were very great to a punishment less than his desert: teaching us even while we suffer to show forth great consideration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having therefore mentioned the ancient law, and recognized it all, He signifies again, that it is not our brother who hath done these deeds, but the evil one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause he hath also subjoined, “But I say unto you, that ye resist not the evil one.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He did not say, “resist not your brother,” but “the evil one,” signifying that on his motion men dare so to act; and in this way relaxing and secretly removing most of our anger against the aggressor, by transferring the blame to another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then?” it is said, “ought we not to resist the evil one?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Indeed we ought, but not in this way, but as He hath commanded, by giving one’s self up to suffer wrongfully; for thus shalt thou prevail over him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For one fire is not quenched by another, but fire by water.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to show thee that even under the old law he that suffered rather prevails, that he it is who wins the crown; examine just what is done, and thou wilt see that his advantage is great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as he that hath begun with unjust acts, will have himself destroyed the eyes of both, his neighbor’s and his own (wherefore also he is justly hated of all, and ten thousand accusations are aimed at him): so he that hath been injured, even after his equal retaliation, will have done nothing horrible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also he hath many to sympathize with him, as being clear from that offense even after he hath retaliated.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And though the calamity be equal to both parties, yet the sentence passed on it is not equal, either with God, or with men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It should seem then, that neither is the calamity equal in the end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now whereas at the beginning He said, “he that is angry with his brother without a cause,” and “he that calleth him fool shall be in danger of hell fire,” here He requires yet more entire self-restraint, commanding him that suffers ill not merely to be quiet, but even to be more exceedingly earnest in his turn,by offering the other cheek.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He saith, not as legislating about such a blow as this only, but as teaching also what forbearance we should practise in all our other trials.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For just as when He saith, “whoso calleth his brother fool, is in danger of hell,” He speaks not of this word only, but also of all reviling; even so here also He is making a law, not so much for our bearing it manfully, when smitten, as that we should be undisturbed, whatever we suffer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because of this He both there singled out the extremest insult, and here hath set down that which seems to be of all blows most opprobrious, the blow on the cheek, so full of all insolence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He commands this as having regard both of him that strikes and of him that is stricken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since both he that is insulted will not think that he suffers any harm, being thus framed to self-restraint (nay, he will not even have any sense of the insult, as striving rather for a prize than as receiving a blow); and he that is offering the affront will be made ashamed, and not add a second blow, though he be fiercer than any wild beast, yea, rather will condemn himself heartily for the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing so restrains the wrong doers, as when the injured bear what is done with gentleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it not only restrains them from rushing onward, but works upon them also to repent for what has gone before, and in wonder at such forbearance to draw back.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it makes them more our own, and causes them to be slaves, not merely friends, instead of haters and enemies; even as avenging one’s self does just the contrary: for it both disgraces each of the two, and makes them worse, and their anger it heightens into a greater flame; yea, often no less than death itself is the end of it, going on from bad to worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He not only forbade thee to be angry when smitten, but even enjoined thee to satiate the other’s desire, that so neither may the former blow appear to have befallen thee against thy will.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus, lost as he may be to shame, thou wilt be able to smite him with a mortal blow, rather than if thou hadst smitten him with thine hand; or if his shamelessness be still greater, thou wilt make him gentle in proportion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not in the matter of blows only, but of our goods also, He would have such forbearance exhibited.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He again employs the same strong figure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, as in the other case He commands to overcome in suffering, so here again, by allowing ourselves to be deprived of more than the wrong doer expected.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, He did not put it so merely, but with something to enhance it: not saying, “give thy cloak to him that asketh,” but “to him that would sue thee at the law,” that is, “if he drag thee into court, and give thee trouble.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And just as, after He had bidden not to call another fool, nor to be angry without cause, He went on and required more, in that He commanded to offer the right cheek also; even so here, having said, “Agree with thine adversary,” He again amplifies the precept.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For now He orders us not only to give what the other would have, but even to show forth a greater liberality.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then!” one may say, “am I to go about naked?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: We should not be naked, if we obeyed these sayings with exactness; rather more abundantly than any should we be clothed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For first, no one would attack men of this disposition; and next, if there chanced to be any one so savage and ungentle, as to proceed even so far, yet many more would be found to clothe him, who acted with such self-denial, not with garments only, but even with their own flesh, if it were possible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Further: even though one were of necessity to go about naked on account of this sort of self-denial, neither so were it any disgrace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since Adam too was “naked”in paradise, “and was not ashamed;” and Isaiah was “naked, and barefoot,” and more glorious than all the Jews;and Josephalso, when he stripped himself, did then more than ever shine forth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to be thus naked is no evil, but to be so clad, as we now are, with costly garments, this is both disgraceful and ridiculous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause, you see, those had praise of God, but these He blames, both by prophets and by apostles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore suppose His injunctions impossible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, for besides their expediency, they are very easy, if we are sober-minded; and the profit of them is so great as to be an exceeding help, not to ourselves only, but to those also who are using us despitefully.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in this chiefly stands their excellence, that while they induce us to suffer wrong, they by the same means teach them also that do the wrong to control themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For while he on his part thinks it a great thing to take what belongs to others, but thou signifiest to him, that to thee it is easy to give even what he doth not ask: while thou bringest in liberality for a counterpoise to his meanness, and a wise moderation to his covetousness: consider what a lesson he will get, being taught not by sayings, but by actual deeds, to scorn vice and to seek after virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For God will have us profitable not to ourselves alone, but to all our neighbors as well.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if thou givest, and abstainest from suing, thou hast sought thine own advantage only; but if thou give him some other thing, thou hast made him too better, and so sent him away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of this nature is salt, which is what He would have them to be; seeing it both recruitsitself, and keeps all other bodies with which it may associate: of this nature is light; for it shows objects both to a man’s self and to all others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as He hath set thee in the rank of these things, help thou likewise him who is sitting in darkness, and teach him that neither before did he take any thing by force: persuade him that he hath done no despite.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for thus thou thyself also wilt be had in more respect and reverence, if thou signify that thou gavest freely and wert not robbed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Make therefore his sin, through thy moderation, an instance of thine own bounty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou think this a great thing, wait, and thou wilt see clearly, that neither yet hast thou attained to perfection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not even here doth He stop with thee, who is laying down the laws of patient endurance, but He proceeds even further, thus saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “If any one shall compel thee to go one mile, go with him twain.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the height of self-denial? in this at least, that after giving thy coat, and thy cloak, not even if thine enemy should wish to use thy naked body for hardships and labors, not even so (saith He), must thou forbid him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He would have us possess all things in common, both our bodies and our goods, as with them that are in need, so with them that insult us: for the latter comes of manliness, the former of mercifulness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because of this, He said, “If any one shall compel thee to go one mile, go with him twain:” again leading thee higher up, and commanding thee to show forth the same kind of ambition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the things of which He spake at the beginning, being far less than these, have so great blessings pronounced on them; consider what sort of portion awaits them, who duly perform these, and what they become even before their rewards, in a human and passiblebody winning entire freedom from passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since when neither insult, nor blows, nor the spoiling of their property, galls them; while they give way to no such thing, but rather add in large measure to their endurance; reflect what kind of training their soul is undergoing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account then, as in regard of blows, as in regard of our goods, so in this case also, He hath bidden us act.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For why,” saith He, “do I mention insult, and property?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though he should want to make use of thy very own limbs for toil and weary work, and this unjustly, do thou again conquer and overpass His unjust desire.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “to compel”is this, to drag unjustly and without any reason, and by way of despite.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, for this also be thou ready in thy station, so as to suffer more than the other would fain do to thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee, turn not thou away.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These last are less than what went before; but marvel not, for this He is ever wont to do, mingling the small with the great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if these be little in comparison with those, let them hearken, who take the goods of others, who distribute their own among harlots, and kindle to themselves a double fire, both by the unrighteous income, and by the pernicious outlay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But by “borrowing,” here, He means not the compact with usury, but the use merely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And elsewhere He even amplifies it, saying that we should give to them, from whom we do not expect to receive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Ye have heard that it hath been said, Thou shalt love thy neighbor, and hate thine enemy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I say unto you, love your enemies, and pray for them which despitefully use you: bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That ye may become likeyour Father which is in Heaven; for He maketh His sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See how He hath set the highest pinnacle on our good deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is why He teaches not only to endure a blow, but to offer the right cheek also; not only to add the cloak to the coat, but to travel also two miles with him who compels thee to go one; in order that thou mightest receive with all facility that which is much more than these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what,” one may say, “is more than these?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not even to count as an enemy him who is doing these things: or rather even somewhat else more than this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not, “do not hate,” but “love;” He said not, “do not injure,” but “do good.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if any one should examine accurately, he will see that even to these things somewhat is added, much greater than they are.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither did He simply command to love, but to pray.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how many steps He hath ascended, and how He hath set us on the very summit of virtue?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, mark it, numbering from the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A first step is, not to begin with injustice: a second, after he hath begun, to vindicate one’s self by equal retaliation; a third, not to do unto him that is vexing us the same that one hath suffered, but to be quiet; a fourth, even to give one’s self up to suffer wrongfully; a fifth, to give up yet more than the other, who did the wrong, wishes; a sixth, not to hate him who hath done so; a seventh, even to love him; an eighth, to do him good also; a ninth, to entreat God Himself on his behalf.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou, what height of self-command?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore glorious too, as we see, is the reward which it hath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, because the thing enjoined was great, and needed a ferventsoul, and much earnestness, He appoints for it also such a reward, as for none of the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He makes not mention here of earth, as with respect to the meek; nor of comfort and mercy, as with regard to the mourners and the merciful; nor of the kingdom of Heaven; but of that which was more thrilling than all; our becoming like God, in such wise as men might become so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He saith, “That ye may become like unto your Father which is in Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And observe, I pray thee, how neither in this place, nor in the preceding parts, doth He call Him His own Father, but in that instance, “God,” and “a great King,” when He was discoursing about oaths, and here, “their Father.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He doth, as reserving for the proper season what He had to say touching these points.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, bringing the likeness yet closer, He saith,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Because He maketh His sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain upon just and unjust.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For He too, so far from hating,” so He speaks, “even pours benefits on those that insult Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely in no respect is the case parallel, not only because of the surpassing nature of His benefits, but also by reason of the excellence of His dignity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou indeed art despised by thy fellow-slave, but He by His slave, who hath also received ten thousand benefits from Him: and thou indeed givest words, in praying for him, but He, deeds, very great and marvellous, kindling the sun, and giving the annual showers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Nevertheless, even so I grant thee to be mine equal, in such wise as it is possible for a man so to be.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hate not then the man that doeth thee wrong, who is procuring thee such good things, and bringing thee to so great honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Curse not him that uses thee despitefully; for so hast thou undergone the labor, but art deprived of the fruit; thou wilt bear the loss, but lose the reward; which is of the utmost folly, having borne the more grievous, not to bear what is less than it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But how,” saith one, “is it possible for this to take place?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having seen God become man, and descend so far, and suffer so much for thy sake, dost thou still inquire and doubt, how it is possible to forgive thy fellow-servants their injuriousness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearest thou not Him on the cross, saying, “Forgive them, for they know not what they do?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearest thou not Paul, when he saith, “He who is gone up on high, and is sitting on the right hand intercedeth for us?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not that even after the cross, and after He had been received up, He sent the apostles unto the Jews that had slain Him, to bring them His ten thousand blessings, and this, though they were to suffer ten thousand terrors at their hands?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hast thou been greatly wronged?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, what hast thou endured like thy Lord, bound, beaten with whips, with rods, spit upon by servants, enduring death, and that death, which is of all deaths the most shameful, after ten thousand favors shown?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And even if thou hast been greatly wronged, for this very cause most of all do thou do him good, that thou mayest both make thine own crown more glorious, and set thy brother free from the worst infirmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so too the physicians, when they are kicked, and shamefully handled by the insane, then most of all pity them, and take measures for their perfect cure, knowing that the insult comes of the extremity of their disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now I bid thee too have the same mind touching them that are plotting against thee, and do thou so treat them that are injuring thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is they above all that are diseased, it is they who are undergoing all the violence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Deliver him then from this grievous contumely, and grant him to let go his anger, and set him free from that grievous demon, wrath.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for if we see persons possessed by devils, we weep for them; we do not seek to be ourselves also possessed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now let us do this too likewise with respect to them that are angry; for in truth the enraged are like the possessed; yea rather, are more wretched than they, being mad with consciousness of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also their frenzy is without excuse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Trample not then on the fallen, but rather pity him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so, should we see any one troubled with bile, blinded and giddy, and straining to cast up this evil humor, we stretch forth a hand, and continue to support him through his struggles, and though we stain our garments, we regard it not, but seek one thing only, how we may set him free from this grievous distress.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then let us do with respect to the angry also, and continue to bear them up when vomiting and struggling; nor let him go, until he put from him all the bitterness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And then shall he feel toward thee the greatest thankfulness; when he is at rest, then he will know clearly from how great trouble thou hast released him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why do I speak of the thanks from him? for God will straightway crown thee, and will requite thee with ten thousand honors, because thou hast freed thy brother from a grievous disease; and that brother too will honor thee as a master, ever reverencing thy forbearance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not the women that are in travail, how they bite those that stand by, and they are not pained? or rather they are pained, but bear it bravely, and sympathize with them who are in sorrow and are torn by those pangs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These do thou too emulate, and prove not softer than women.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after these women have brought forth (for these men are more feeble minded than women), then they will know thee to be a man in comparison.of Heaven?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then can we deserve, who are commanded to emulate God, and are perhaps in a way not so much as to equal the publicans?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if “to love them that love us” be the part of publicans, sinners, and heathens: when we do not even this (and we do it not, so long as we envy our brethren who are in honor), what penalty shall we not incur, commanded as we are to surpass the scribes, and taking our place below the heathens?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then shall we behold the kingdom, I pray thee? how shall we set foot on that holy threshold, who are not surpassing even the publicans?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this He covertly signified, when He said, “Do not even the publicans the same?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this thing most especially we may admire in His teaching, that while in each instance He sets down with very great fullness the prizes of the conflicts; such as “to see God,” and “to inherit the kingdom of Heaven,” and “to become sons of God,” and “like God,” and “to obtain mercy,” and “to be comforted,” and “the great reward:” if anywhere He must needs mention things grievous, He doth this in a subdued tone.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus in the first place, the name of hell He hath set down once only in so many sentences; and in some other instances too, it is with reserve that He corrects the hearer, and as though he were managing His discourse rather in the way of shaming than threatening him; where He saith, “do not even the publicans the same?” and, “if the salt have lost its savor;” and, “he shall be called least in the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And there are places where He puts down the sin itself by way of punishment, leaving to the hearer to infer the grievousness of the punishment: as when He saith, “he hath committed adultery with her in his heart;” and, “he that putteth away causeth her to commit adultery;” and, “That which is more than these is of the evil one.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to them that have understanding, instead of the mention of the punishment, the very greatness of the sin is sufficient for correction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He here brings forward the heathens and the publicans, by the quality of the person putting the disciple to shame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which Paul too did, saying, “Sorrow not, even as the rest which have no hope;”and, “Even as the Gentiles which know not God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to signify that He requires nothing very overpowering, but a little more than was accustomed, He saith,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Do not even the Gentilesthe same?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet nevertheless He stops not the discourse at this, but makes it end with His rewards, and those good hopes, saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Be ye therefore perfect, as your Heavenly Father.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He intersperses everywhere abundantly the name of the heavens, by the very place thoroughly elevating their minds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as yet, I know not how, they were somewhat weak and dull.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then, bearing in mind all the things which have been said, show forth great love even towards our enemies; and let us cast away that ridiculous custom, to which many of the more thoughtless give way, waiting for those that meet them to address them first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Towards that which hath a great blessing, they have no zeal; but what is ridiculous, that they follow after.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore now dost thou not address him first?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Because he is waiting for this,” is the reply.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, for this very reason most of all thou shouldest have sprung forward to him, that thou mightest win the crown.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “No,” saith he, “since this was his object.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what can be worse than this folly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, “Because this,” saith he, “was his object;—to become procurer of a reward for me;—I will not put my hand to what he has thus suggested.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if he first address thee, thou gainest nothing, even though thou accost him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou be first to spring forward and speak to him, thou hast made thyself profit of his pride, and hast gathered in a manner abundant fruit from his obstinacy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What is it then but the utmost folly, when we are to reap so large fruit from bare words, to give up the gain; and condemning him, to stumble at the very same thing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou blamest him for this, that he first waits to be addressed by another, wherefore dost thou emulate that same thing which thou accusest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That which thou saidst was evil, why art thou to imitate the same as good?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how that nothing is more senseless than a man who associates with wickedness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, I entreat, let us flee this evil and ridiculous practice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for ten thousand friendships hath this pestilence overthrown, many enmities hath it wrought.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause then let us anticipate them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since we who are commanded to take blows, and be compelled to journey,and to be stripped by enemies, and to bear it; what kind of indulgence should we deserve, exhibiting so great contentiousness in a mere formal address?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why,” saith one, “we are despised and spit upon, the moment we have given him up this.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in order that man may not despise thee, dost thou offend God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in order that thy frenzied fellow servant may not despise thee, dost thou despise the Lord, who hath bestowed on thee benefits so great?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, if it be amiss that thine equal should despise thee, how much more that thou shouldest despise the God that made thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And together with this, consider that other point also; that when he despises thee, he is at that very moment employed in procuring to thee a greater reward.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since for God’s sake thou submittest to it, because thou hast hearkened to His laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this, to what kind of honor is it not equal? to how many diadems?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be it my portion both to be insulted and despised for God’s sake, rather than to be honored by all kings; for nothing, nothing is equal to this glory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then let us pursue, in such wise as Himself commanded, and making no account of the things of men, but showing forth perfect self restraint in all things, let us so direct our own lives.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so even now, from this very time, we shall enjoy the good things of the heavens, and of the crowns that are there, walking as angels among men, going about in the earth like the angelic powers, and abiding apart from all lust, from all turmoil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And together with all these things we shall receive also the unutterable blessings: unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory, and power, and worship, with the unoriginate Father, and the Holy and Good Spirit, now and always, even forever and ever.
